video_title: The Case for Sunshine (with Rowan Jacobsen) 6/15/26
gist: Author Rowan Jacobsen argues the decades-long public-health message that all sun exposure is dangerous is outdated: skin cancer kills few people, vitamin D pills fail in trials, and moderate sun exposure appears to lower cardiovascular and cancer mortality via mechanisms like nitric oxide, implying a large net health trade-off in favor of sun.

themes.theme: Skin cancer incidence vs. mortality and overdiagnosis

insights.claim: Roughly 98% of skin cancer cases are basal or squamous cell carcinomas that rarely become invasive and carry negligible mortality; only about 2% are melanoma, the type with meaningful death risk.
insights.speaker: Rowan Jacobsen
insights.so_what: The headline skin-cancer scare conflates a mostly benign condition with a rare lethal one, distorting risk perception.
insights.quote: those are responsible for about 98% of all skin cancer cases. Then the third important type of skin cancer is melanoma and that's more like 2%

insights.claim: The single best predictor of a US skin-cancer diagnosis is the number of dermatologists in your county and the local age bracket, not sun exposure, indicating diagnosis is supply-driven.
insights.speaker: Rowan Jacobsen
insights.so_what: Rising skin-cancer 'incidence' largely reflects more looking, not more disease, a classic supplier-induced detection dynamic.
insights.quote: How many dermatologists there are in your county and and the overall age bracket for people in your county.

insights.claim: Melanoma mortality has stayed flat decade after decade while incidence rises steeply, which experts cite as the classic statistical signature of overdiagnosis rather than a real epidemic.
insights.speaker: Rowan Jacobsen
insights.so_what: Flat mortality against rising incidence undercuts the narrative of a genuine, worsening sun-driven cancer crisis.
insights.quote: mortality is totally flat while incidence is rising steeply. And as some scientists have pointed out, that's kind of like like the classic sign of overdiagnosis.

insights.claim: In a re-read study, identical melanoma slides judged as 11 melanomas 20 years ago were judged as 18 melanomas today by the same and other dermatologists, evidencing diagnostic drift toward calling more lesions cancer.
insights.speaker: Rowan Jacobsen
insights.so_what: Diagnostic thresholds, not biology, are inflating cancer statistics used to justify sun avoidance.
insights.quote: 20 years ago 11 were called were judged to be melanomas and today the exact same slides 18 of them were judged to be melanomas.

insights.claim: Skin cancer kills about 10,000 Americans a year versus roughly 1 million from cardiovascular disease and 600,000 from cancer, and it does not rank in the world's top 40 causes of death.
insights.speaker: Rowan Jacobsen
insights.so_what: Any policy that trades a tiny skin-cancer risk against the giant killers should weight the giants heavily.
insights.quote: in the US, it causes, you know, about 10,000 people a year die from skin cancer... cardiovascular disease is, you know, closer to a million people and cancer is like maybe 600,000 people.

themes.theme: Sunscreen efficacy and the UVA generation gap

insights.claim: Rates of skin cancer and sunscreen use have tracked each other closely for decades, and epidemiological studies repeatedly find more sunscreen use correlated with more skin cancer.
insights.speaker: Rowan Jacobsen
insights.so_what: Sunscreen has not delivered the protection its marketing implies, though reverse causation partly explains the correlation.
insights.quote: rates of skin cancer and rates of sunscreen use track each other spectacularly well

insights.claim: SPF measures only UVB (burn) protection, so early SPF sunscreens let large doses of UVA through while suppressing the burn signal, plausibly making them worse than nothing by extending exposure time 10-30x.
insights.speaker: Rowan Jacobsen
insights.so_what: A trusted safety metric measured the wrong thing, encouraging behavior that likely raised melanoma risk.
insights.quote: by blocking sunburn and making you think you were safe from skin cancer they were allowing you to stay in the sun depending on the SPF 10 15 20 30 times longer. And all of that time gobs of UVA was pouring into your body

insights.claim: US sunscreens are roughly a generation behind those sold in Europe, Asia, and Israel, with better broad-spectrum formulations expected to reach the US market starting next year.
insights.speaker: Rowan Jacobsen
insights.so_what: A regulatory lag has left US consumers with inferior sun protection and signals a coming product refresh in the US market.
insights.quote: The the sunscreens are used in in Europe and uh Asia and like Israel are much better than the ones that are allowed in the US.

insights.claim: Observational studies show sunscreen effects improving over time: no benefit or reverse effect for 1970s products, roughly a wash in the 1980s-90s, and net positive for modern broad-spectrum formulas.
insights.speaker: Rowan Jacobsen
insights.so_what: Product quality, not sunscreen per se, drives outcomes, so blanket historical claims about sunscreen are misleading.
insights.quote: the early ones from like the 70s show no effect and and often you know reverse effect... And now more the more modern ones it's starting to look better for sunscreen now.

themes.theme: Vitamin D: supplement failure vs. natural sun exposure

insights.claim: Large rigorous placebo-controlled vitamin D trials have failed across the board: raising blood vitamin D via pills does not help cancer, cardiovascular, autoimmune, or other conditions unless the person is extremely deficient.
insights.speaker: Rowan Jacobsen
insights.so_what: The most-prescribed supplement in the world lacks trial support, exposing a gap between association and causation.
insights.quote: They have completely failed across the board, where we know that unless you are extremely vitamin D deficient, raising your the amount in your blood through pill doesn't help with any of these conditions.

insights.claim: New England Journal of Medicine editors have publicly urged doctors to stop prescribing vitamin D pills, yet it remains the most prescribed supplement in the world.
insights.speaker: Rowan Jacobsen
insights.so_what: Persistent prescribing despite negative evidence shows how sticky medical habits and placebo value keep a market alive.
insights.quote: the editors of the New England Journal of Medicine have published editorials saying, "Stop prescribing vitamin D pills. It's not not helping."

insights.claim: Vitamin D may be merely a biomarker of sun exposure rather than the causal agent of sunlight's health benefits, explaining why natural high-D populations are healthy while pill-raised D provides no benefit.
insights.speaker: Rowan Jacobsen
insights.so_what: If D is a proxy, the entire supplement-based public-health strategy targets the wrong variable.
insights.quote: whether it was just sort of along for the ride. You know, it was it was an indicator of sun exposure, but it wasn't the agent that was bringing the good health

insights.claim: The body's vitamin D production halves by age 70 relative to the 30s for the same sunlight, and it must be made near solar noon (roughly 1-3pm), limiting how much older adults can self-correct via sun.
insights.speaker: Rowan Jacobsen
insights.so_what: Sun-based vitamin D strategies are age- and timing-dependent, weakening simple 'just get sun' advice for the elderly.
insights.quote: by the time we're 70, we're on we're only producing half as much vitamin D for the same amount of sunlight as when we're, you know, in our 30s.

insights.claim: Dermatologist Richard Weller showed that shining ~20-25 minutes of summer-equivalent sunlight on skin raises nitric oxide and lowers blood pressure by 5-6 points, a mechanism independent of vitamin D.
insights.speaker: Rowan Jacobsen
insights.so_what: A plausible non-D mechanism links sun to cardiovascular benefit, strengthening the case that sun's value is real.
insights.quote: shining it on their arm would lower their blood pressure. It raised nitric oxide in their blood... and lowered blood pressure by like five or six points.

insights.claim: A Weller UK Biobank preprint tracking ~420,000 people for ~20 years found high-sun individuals about 20% more likely to die of melanoma but far less likely to die of cardiovascular disease and cancer, a roughly 75-to-1 lives-saved ratio.
insights.speaker: Rowan Jacobsen
insights.so_what: If robust, the trade-off math overwhelmingly favors more sun exposure at the population level.
insights.quote: the ratio was 75 to 1 that more more light for every one extra death from melanoma that it created was saving about 75 deaths uh, from other bigger more problematic diseases.

insights.claim: Of ~420,000 UK Biobank participants, ~45,000 died over the period but only 440 from melanoma and 60 from other skin cancers, quantifying how minor skin-cancer mortality is relative to total deaths.
insights.speaker: Rowan Jacobsen
insights.so_what: Skin cancer accounts for about 1% of deaths in this cohort, reframing it as a small line item in overall mortality.
insights.quote: Only 440 had died of melanoma. Only 60 had died of other skin cancers out of 420,000 people.

insights.claim: Both the case that sunlight causes skin cancer and the case that it prevents other diseases rest on observational data, since ethics and funding preclude randomized sun-exposure trials in either direction.
insights.speaker: Rowan Jacobsen
insights.so_what: The evidentiary asymmetry investors and policymakers assume (proven harm vs. speculative benefit) does not actually exist.
insights.quote: the reasons that we believe that... sunlight it causes skin cancer are also from observational studies.

themes.theme: Historical arc and epistemics of public-health advice

insights.claim: In the late 1800s tuberculosis caused roughly one in four European deaths, and Faroe Islands doctor Finsen's discovery that concentrated sunlight cured cutaneous TB, plus sunlight curing rickets via vitamin D, launched the heliotherapy era.
insights.speaker: Rowan Jacobsen
insights.so_what: Sunlight was once mainstream medicine, showing how completely a health consensus can reverse.
insights.quote: one out of every four deaths in Europe was due to tuberculosis in the later 1800s.

insights.claim: US farming fell from about 40% of the workforce in 1900 to 3% by century's end, so the population moved indoors even as skin-cancer rates exploded, making rising sun exposure an implausible sole cause.
insights.speaker: Russ Roberts
insights.so_what: The dominant sun-causes-cancer story ignores that measured outdoor time fell over the same period.
insights.quote: farming goes from 40% of of the workforce in 1900 to 3% by the end of the century. Uh so people aren't in the sun.

insights.claim: Roberts frames observational epidemiology as an 'intellectual cesspool' where an individual raising a biomarker to match healthy populations may not gain their outcomes because unmeasured confounders (latitude, income, diet, genetics) are not controlled.
insights.speaker: Russ Roberts
insights.so_what: A durable mental model for discounting correlation-based health and market claims where confounders dominate.
insights.quote: these observational studies in epidemiology, which are um in my view a cesspool, intellectual cesspool

insights.claim: Jacobsen argues LLMs now let laypeople locate foundational research directly by asking for studies and following the cited primary sources, changing how non-experts can audit expert consensus.
insights.speaker: Rowan Jacobsen
insights.so_what: AI-assisted source retrieval erodes the information asymmetry that lets institutions maintain contested health narratives.
insights.quote: we're now in this kind of amazing era where we have these incredibly powerful research assistants on hand all the time, you know, the LLMs that have read everything

insights.claim: Jacobsen's actionable rule is to avoid both zero sun and any burning, since zero exposure is clearly unhealthy and a burn signals cell death, with tolerance scaling to skin tone: darker skin can take far more, red-haired fair skin is most melanoma-prone.
insights.speaker: Rowan Jacobsen
insights.so_what: Blanket sun-avoidance guidance written for the fairest skin over-restricts the majority who could safely get more sun.
insights.quote: zero sun exposure is not healthy for people. It's also clear... that burning is bad.
